speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape three of an interview with Lisbeth Kornreich.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is how I spent the first night, worrying about my mother above all. Nothing I could do. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Early up, which is fine. I won't have these skirts hanging over my head anymore when any one of those people needed to go. And so they picked up the straw mattresses. And the Jehovah's Witness lady flipped back her bed. And there was more space. And there was-- which I had not known in the dusk and in the dark-- there was also a table on the other wall flipped up, which was brought down. And oh, you would say, like, a picnic table and a picnic bench, you know-- two benches alongside the table. So there was a place to sit, and a place to-- to lean. And I made myself as thin as I could and slid to the wall and just caught up on my physical strength. And then they brought some soup and some beans. I don't recall what it-- it was something. It was warm. It was in a bowl, and it had a spoon, and I ate it, of course. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And so the days went by. And the conversation never included me, except at one time the Jehovah's Witness lady told me how she had gotten there-- and she told me because she probably told any  one of the others a number of times, so she had a new audience-- that she was doing the wash, and somebody came and took her to where she was now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so what was happening to the wash? Because the wash can't soak forever, and also, the others need the-- the-- the laundry room. And they probably threw her wash down on the floor. And worse yet, they might even steal it. And she was-- I was concerned about my mother. She was concerned about her wash.

speaker: interviewee
text: Both concerns were very legitimate, but this was one person who had said I was human and who even told me why she was there. And the others were there, it turned out, one for prostitution and one for black marketing and one for something else again. But basically, they were harmless and kept amongst themselves. But they did say that, Friday, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] kommt der gruene Heinrich. The 'Green Heinrich' comes on Friday. The Green Heinrich is what you call here the 'Black Maria.' And then they either go to a regular court of law, or they got to get a hearing or a trial, or they're being released, or whatever happens. But, of course, the Gruene Heinrich also brings you to a place from where you go to Dachau. Dachau was a concentration camp. In those days, we didn't know about-- well, actually, the-- the extermination camps didn't exist. If they existed, they existed as camps. But they didn't have the installation for the Final Solution.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't have the gas chambers, any of that. But it was bad enough. Anyway, if you are in Vienna, you go to Dachau. You know? If you go to the-- if you you go to a camp, this would have been Dachau, which was in Bavaria. Anyhow, so all I had to do was wait for Friday and see what happens. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Friday came, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and somebody opened-- oh, I have to say before then, in-- to be truthful and give them their due, we were taken to the shower. And I was able to shower, which is nice. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Friday came. Somebody opened the door and said, [LAUGHS] their regular way, Modley, mittkommen! And it's Friday. And Modley came and was brought down to the-- to the office, was given her belt, her hat, and her gloves. I think I had a watch or something, yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And with no words-- nothing to sign, nothing sign in, nothing sign out. And there I stood in the office. And whoever gave me my things walked out of the office. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And there I stood for a while. And then I went to the door and said to myself, There must be somebody somewhere, some explanation. I stuck my nose out the door. I didn't see the Gruene Heinrich. The Black Maria wasn't there. Well, maybe the Black Maria is parked outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took another few steps. Any time, I could have been shot in the back trying to flee. God knows what. I couldn't just stand there. So I walked another few steps. And by then, I was at the-- at the main door, at the main entrance. There was no guard, no nothing. One of my many-- thanks God-- many friends of all persuasions and religions, who had fake papers so he was not Jewish, was standing there a little off to the side. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Heinz! Liesl. I said, can-- can I go now? Yeah, you're free. How do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, anyway, he had gotten in touch with my mother. And they had gotten in touch with the lawyer. And the lawyer had found out that the Zollfahndungsstelle had gotten me. And when they found out that Zollfahndungsstelle had gotten me, they knew it was in connection with the lift. And I think by then, they knew my mother had the power of attorney. And she signed it they can't-- I don't know if she signed anything that they can open the lift or whatnot. Whatever it was, I was free and clear. But nobody told me I can go home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Heinz had brought me a roll with something on it. Now, this all was on cards and-- and coupons, of course. Come, eat, eat, eat. I said, it's the middle of the afternoon. I am not hungry. How come you're not hungry? You must be hungry. You couldn't have eaten. You were in there a whole week. I said, well, it's what they gave me. They didn't let us starve. It wasn't much. It wasn't good. But I ate. You ate that? I don't know whether it should be on tape, because there comes a classical expression. And this young man looked at me and said, and you probably didn't sleep all this time. I said, yes, I slept, and yes, I ate.

speaker: interviewee
text: You are not a woman. You are a horse! [LAUGHS] It shouldn't be on the tape. But we often laughed about it, that this really what kept me alive, maybe that I did sleep and I did eat when given the opportunity. So anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after that, they had all, then, if they needed anything, to confiscate the lift then. So the lift then was confiscated. And that's what it was really all about. I had-- I have something to add to when the concierge wanted to get even and told that the landlady-- remember way back, that the landlady was listening to foreign broadcasts?

speaker: interviewee
text: And there came somebody and took me to SS headquarters, again for a deposition. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they wanted to know if the landlady listened to foreign broadcasts. By then, we were in-- in the middle of the war. And-- because if she did, she would probably tell the Jews. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No, she hadn't told us, so I can't say. But I think she is such a coward, she probably never listens to anything that's not permitted. And that was that. And that black-uniformed SS person said to his typist, now [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] type this up and bring it back for a signature. And when the other person left the room, he turned to me-- because at one time I told you that the Austrian Nazis were not so happy. He turned to me and said, I risked everything to be in the underground. And now all my superiors are from Germany. But I do have enough information. I know that very, very bad things are going to happen. Believe me. I'm a fellow Austrian. You're Austrian, and I am Austrian, and I never thought it would come to that. Go over what we call the [GERMAN], over the green borders through the fields, through the woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: But go, and go this week. Go as fast as you can. Don't wait for legal immigration. Go. Go over any kind of border that you can run across. Any kind-- it was Switzerland by then. Everything else was Germany. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that, I think, was a very, very decent gesture. I couldn't go any place, because what would I do with mother? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But on the other hand, I must say, for the dear Austrians, when you think of it, the real, innate antisemitism comes from Austria. That's not learned. That comes with mother's milk, starting with Hitler and Eichmann and Kaltenbrunner. The worst of the lot, they were Austrians. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyhow, so this was a nice guy. But he forgot to stamp my-- my piece of paper so I could leave the headquarters. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This typist came in. I signed that this is-- this is what I had to say. And then he left, and she left, and I was again in an office with no place to go. And I went out in the corridor, and I had no exit slip. And there was-- I couldn't find him anymore. He'd gone to lunch, whatever. There was a door ajar to an office. And I knocked and I said, excuse me, please, but I-- I was in office 34 and the Obersturmscharfuhrer, da, da, da forgot to stamp my card here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this person who was at the desk didn't even look up. And I gave him the card, and he stamped it, and I walked out. And this person was Eichmann. Yeah. And little did he know, or did I know. So. Tell me about how it was that Viennese Jews ended up in Jew houses. How was-- how did that come about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, the good places, the villas or whatnot, they were occupied immediately. And then they-- there was a great shortage of housing in Vienna all along. I mean, young people couldn't get married because they had no place to live. They had to live with papa, mama. Or they got married and moved in with papa.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was a great need, easily filled. You throw the Jews out. Now, that particular house that we were sent to was on a very prestigious street and a very big, beautiful house, with a large courtyard and a wing in the back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the front was all occupied by Gentiles. And they brought as many Jews-- there were Jews living there already. This was a fairly Jewish section. And they brought as many Jews as they could into the existing apartments of the Jews who lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was-- we were sent to a very nice old lady and her spinster daughter, who had a-- an apartment of three rooms. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So they got to keep one room. Another room, a Jewish journalist was sent to. And then there was a room right off the corridor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now this is four flights up, a big stone staircase-- cold-- and mother and I got that room, which used to be their dining room because there was still a great, big [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] breakfront in there. And we got two [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] makeshift beds. Unfortunately, there were a lot of bedbugs, not only in those beds, but in the building. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't know whether that was worse, or the fact that it was under the roof and the roof leaked, so that we had to go to bed on rainy days with the umbrella open.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the assignment of Jews to these so-called apartments done by the Nazis, or by the Jewish organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, no, no, no, no, no. This was strictly a Nazi thing. No, the Jewish community had another terrible task. I'll tell you about that. No, we got this card that we have to leave the apartment in 24 hours. We had to go to city hall. And one of the largest halls in city hall had a desk at one end, and the rest was taken up with people standing in line who also had gotten their eviction notice. And this man at the desk had a list of places where one could still push a few Jews in. And, of course, at 4:00 or 5:00 or whenever he stopped, the line stopped, and these people had to come back the next day. Now, this was a very scary thing because you really-- you never knew what are they going to follow up on and whatnot. And one didn't want to take too many chances. So if they say, you should be out of there, maybe the Aryan tenant is coming in right then and there, and you're still there, and God knows what happens. On the other hand, they might never follow up, and they just have a vacancy, and it's there. So I don't know what would have happened if we had stayed there. But fortunately, we were still-- now, if you get out of line-- I hope you have no physical need to get out of line-- you can't ever get back. You go to the end of the line again. And some very bad things happened there, too. Well, anyhow, we were fortunate. We got-- and then we got the ticket with the address, and Mama and I went there. And that was that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The house doesn't stand anymore. It got the full blast. I mean, there is a house there now, but not-- not the original one. And so we were there for a couple of years.

speaker: interviewer
text: In that same room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-huh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you said that the Jewish community did something else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. When they had a railroad car to fill, to go to Dachau or wherever, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Jewish community had the honor of providing the names of those that go. We need 30 more, or 50. I mean, they-- they collected enough. But they still had a few places in the railroad car to fill it up. And the Jewish community had to provide the names, which was a heart-breaker. I don't have to tell you that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Horrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went up to the Jewish community for some kind of a document or something. And somebody said they were absolutely crying because he had to put down the name of his former teacher. That was an old lady, no-- no-- you know, no family being pulled apart or whatnot. And he had to. I mean, if not, he had to go. You know, things like that. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, let's see, when did you leave Vienna for the US? It was after-- your father had gone on?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had gone to England. My mother and I had not been able to leave when we thought we would. We were there without winter coats and without much of anything else. We were in the Jew house. I told-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, in June '41, but how did we survive? OK. I told you that the lift van was confiscated. My mother was always very capable in design. And she designed my ball gowns and what have you, and she could sew very well and embroider and all of this sort of thing. And she had a very good sewing machine, a Singer that could do everything and anything, which was also in the lift van. We had an uncle who had emigrated to Holland, and who sent us a box containing a big Edam cheese. Mmmm. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now, the lift van was being auctioned off, or distributed to high-ranking Nazis or former underground [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] heroes, whatever-- to a select group of party members. And that was done in an auction house on the outskirts of town. And the auction houses were state-owned. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I-- we knew when that would happen. Now came this big Edam. And my mother and I both knew-- I mean, that's never going to cross our lips. I mean, this is too valuable. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when you need it, there is-- believe in God, don't believe in God, somehow, something happens. So I took the Edam, and I went out to the outskirts of town to that auction house to the back entrance. I had no plan. Don't think that I'm so brilliant that I know what I'm doing. This was pure instinct.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I went to that auction house, to the back room, to the office. And there was a-- all of a sudden, I don't know English. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What do you call that, sort of a half-wall between the public and the people who-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what do you call that? I'm trying to-- I'm trying to think. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Does it have a special name, other than the way you describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, like the-- the-- like, in a bar, you know that-- it's not a table. It's not a shelf. It's-- anyway, there were the-- the-- the people who made lists or did things. And there was the public. And in between was a half-wall with a shelf on top, all right?

speaker: interviewer
text: So the public is here. The others are there, like you have in some drugstores. And they are so happy that now it's lower so we can talk to the druggists. That kind of a partition. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there I am with my box with the Edam. And I put that box on that partition. And, as I told you-- I mean, I was born, raised in Vienna. And I spoke Viennese just like anybody else. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I said, so, the Modley van is now being emptied out? Uh-huh. Don't forget, that the box was stayed there. And I said, gee. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Frau Modley had a sewing machine. That's probably there, too. Now, they emptied out. You know, the paintings went, the furniture went, and the clothes went. I was in the back from where they take it to the stage. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then there was the sewing machine. I said, God, that sewing machine. You know, that's our livelihood, that sewing machine. I just wonder what's going to happen. I says, happen with that sewing machine. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] have an Edam here. Now, you have to realize that food was on stamps. Even for the Gentiles during the war, it wasn't-- it was no different. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My uncle sent that, but we would never eat that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] To make the story short, he got the Edam, and I got the sewing machine, and I went out the back entrance. And here I am, at the outskirts of town. This is one of my daughter Carol's favorite stories. [LAUGHS] I'm at the out-- at the very edge of town, in a very low-class neighborhood, on a sidewalk, as a Jew. Of course, you know, in those days, we didn't have the star yet-- no.

speaker: interviewer
text: But whatever. I'm on the sidewalk with a sewing machine. A big sewing machine, built-in, you know, with-- with-- in a chest and with-- with the shelves so we could-- you know. It was a big piece of furniture, relatively speaking. [SIGHS] How do I get it from here to there? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It so happened that-- as I said, we had so many good friends of various denominations. We had a friend who-- always thought she was Aryan, was brought up Catholic, was in love with a man who joined the party, thought she could marry him. But she was a friend. And when she wanted to marry him, it turned out that her grandparents had been baptized, but actually, they were Jews, which she never knew, and which she apologized. And she really didn't know. Mrs. [? Albright-- ?] you know, I mean, this is not unusual. And so she was considered a half-Aryan, whatever. She could keep her firm. She could keep everything. She raised her-- everything was fine, except she couldn't marry a Nazi functionary, and she couldn't do several things. But she had two strong brothers with the same background who served in the army. I mean, they had all the obligations of full Aryans, but none of the-- not all of the privileges.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I knew that her name was also Liesl, of course, so I knew that she had these strong brothers, and they could possibly do something with this heavy sewing machine. So I called from the street, telephone, with the sewing machine standing on the sidewalk. If somebody would want to take it, take it. I mean, I wouldn't make any fuss over it. I wouldn't say that it belongs to me, whatever. I called her, and I said, Liesl, I am out in Floretzdorf with a sewing machine. Can you get ahold of your brothers? Would they help me? Oh, sure. Just stay where you are. Tell me where you are, corner such and so. And stay where you are and wait. It was a long wait. It was over an hour, maybe more.

speaker: interviewee
text: And under the circumstances, you know, I always tried to go away from the sewing machine so they should connect me with it, and then come back to the sewing machine so they shouldn't think it's free for grabs. But since this was, as I said, outskirts, and nothing much happened there, nobody made any attempt. People-- very few people came and went and paid no attention. And then those two brothers came-- those two strong brothers came and dragged that sewing machine through town to where we lived, and brought it up four flights. And now we had the sewing machine, and this is how we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were expecting all along to join your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We were waiting and waiting and hoping. You couldn't go-- see, Vienna was no longer a-- Berlin was the capital. Vienna no longer had the embassy. So the American consulate was in Vienna. The American consulate was staffed by America Firsters. And they were all very proud of that. I had friends who lived in the same area, in the-- in the embassy area, and had the same grocers and butchers and whatnot. And these wives and mothers went there and bragged that my son is in a position to keep the riffraff out of-- of the United States. And so you could only go there when you were invited on such a day, at such an hour. It was impossible. You could write to them-- it went into a wastebasket. You never got an answer. When they were good and ready to call you, you came. So finally, they called us, and decided that my cousin's affidavit wasn't strong enough. There was one postponement after the other. And I-- while we were there, we saw how families were torn apart. Now, Vienna is a very windy city in the spring. There's a lot of-- of dust from the cobblestones. And conjunctivitis-- eye inflammation, red eyes-- is not unusual at all. So one of the children had a little eye inflammation. They said, could be glaucoma. We can't give you a visa. And all the papers ran out for these people. And this little boy was supposed to bring all kinds of-- of doctors' affidavits and whatnot and come back in six weeks or some such thing. In other words, this family was condemned to stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everybody had their little-- everybody cooperated a little bit in the tragedy. So finally, we got our visa. Good. OK. We're going to take a break for changing the tape.
